data.title: Navitas: Not A Value Play, But I'm All In
data.description:
I initiate my coverage on Navitas Semiconductor Corporation (NASDAQ:NVTS) with a Strong Buy rating, cemented by three main pillars.

First, the share price is still holding up after the announcement of the partnership with Nvidia (NVDA) on their next generation 800 V HVDC architecture, which will support the Rubin Ultra, expected in 2027.

That was two weeks ago, more than enough time for the stock to selloff back to pre announcement levels. Sorry for conservative value investors, but it all points out that hype is back as the S&P 500 (SP500) is bouncing back to reach all time highs.

Second, the fundamentals don't look that bad. They are not amazing, but we are talking about a debt free company, with a quarterly 8 digit constant revenue stream, selling power chips to all top 10 mobile OEMs and pulling consistent double-digit gross margins. From a dilution perspective, the situation has drastically improved from 2023 and stock based compensation went down significantly in the past two quarters. Essentially, the company has enough cash until the end of 2026, when the Rubin Ultra is set to be generally available to hyperscalers. Which brings me to the third pillar.

Third, the collaboration with Nvidia could pivot the company away from supplying power chips for consumer smartphone chargers (where there is an industry-wide slowdown) towards high margin AI accelerators.

I don't have a price target in mind, but I do see enough upside to justify my bet on this stock. That doesn't mean there aren't risks. In fact this is a highly risky bet, just like all the positions I own in my two portfolios.

My position in Navitas is about 8% of my portfolio's liquidation value.

Company Overview

This is a California-based company, founded in 2014. It started trading in October 2021 after a SPAC with Live Oak Acquisition Corp. II. It has a market cap of $1.14 billion and an enterprise value of $1.07 billion, with $75 million in cash and $6.9 million in total debt. Please note that the company is debt-free, and the $6.9 million in total debt comes from capital leases.

In plain English, Navitas makes special chips that help electronic devices use power more efficiently. Their gallium nitride (GaN) and silicon carbide (SiC) power chips are mainly used in chargers for smartphones.

What I found surprising is the fact that the company’s GaN chips are in mass production for all 10 of the top 10 global mobile OEMs. From the latest 10-Q (emphasis added):

We consider ourselves to be a pioneer in the GaN market with a proprietary, proven GaN power IC platform that is shipping in mass production to tier-1 companies including Samsung, Dell, Lenovo, LG, Xiaomi, OPPO, Amazon, vivo and Motorola.

I now wonder if the fast charger of my Samsung mobile is using one of their chips.

Just like Nvidia, the company uses a fabless model, where they simply design their chips and partner with third party suppliers and manufacturers to build and test them.

Based on the latest 10-Q and this PR, I believe TSMC could be their main partner manufacturer (emphasis added):

A significant amount of the Company’s third-party subcontractors and suppliers, including the third-party foundry that supplies wafers for GaN ICs, are located in Taiwan.

A significant amount of the Company’s assembly and test operations are conducted by third-party contractors in Taiwan and the Philippines.

In my view, the company has a decent MOAT. Its most important proprietary advantage is a process design kit used as a guide by engineers to design new GaN circuits. Is this PDK strong enough to differentiate the company from competitors like giant ON Semiconductor (ON)? Probably not, but still you can't overlook the fact that Navitas' GaN chips are used by the top 10 mobile OEMs around the globe. This is not your day to day R&D penny stock surviving only its narrative. As seen below, the company has a decent recurring revenue stream.

That said, about 90% of the revenue in the last quarter was used in R&D in both its U.S. and China labs. Yikes!

Fundamentals

Let's look at the fundamentals now.

I'd like to start with a more granular view of revenue and gross profits.

At first sight, the trend for the past 12 months looks unfavorable. In the last earnings call, CFO Todd Glickman noted the following drives for the decline:

The decline compared to a year ago quarter was primarily the result of lower revenues in the EV and solar markets.

Call me crazy, but I think the overall slowdown in the (consumer) semiconductor industry may have been the main driver for the decline in revenue. People are not buying smartphones, so they are not buying the new fast chargers. Sales for the latest Samsung model are below expectations and a similar pattern can be found for Apple's latest model.

What I found surprising is that, even in this industry-wide downturn, the company is pulling positive gross margins, even in Q4 last year, when it recorded $5 million in an inventory write-down due to the termination of its relationship with a major distributor.

By the way, that same distributor accounted for 56% of Navitas' net revenues in 2024. Is this concerning?

Not to me, as in the latest 10-Q, I was able to see another new distributor, which accounts for 51% of revenue.

How about operating and net margins?

Yikes! As I said earlier, Navitas burned 90% of its Q1 revenue in R&D.

I know where you're going next: SG&A (in other words, stock-based compensation). Well, it turns out that the SG&A expenses decreased in the last quarter by 27% YOY.

A quick look at the cash flow statement shows SBC declining significantly for the past two quarters, which is great news for the few investors who don't like companies with high stock-based compensation, or SBC, while being unprofitable (I'm not one of them).

Speaking of profitability, the table below doesn't look particularly exciting.

The company is burning 8 figures per quarter. Considering the $75 million in cash and ST investments, and a cash burn rate of $13 million per quarter, the company has a cash runway of approximately one and a half years before it needs a new infusion of capital (either traditional debt or equity financing).

Speaking of dilution, the chart below shows the quarterly increase in the total nr of outstanding shares.

Since Q2 2024, the company has been diluting investors at a rate of 5% per quarter. That's significantly less than the rate in 2023.

So, what's my verdict so far?

Meh.

The company is able to stay afloat, and I believe if cash is straining, they will cut down their R&D spending. To put that figure into perspective, the R&D expense in the past quarter was $12.7 million while the operating cash outflow was $13.5 million. Cutting down R&D brings the company quite close to the break even point.

However, the company needs something more exciting to see its share price take off. This brings me to the next point.

The Deal With Nvidia

On May 21, Navitas announced a collaboration with Nvidia for "their next-generation 800 V HVDC architecture to support ‘Kyber’ rack-scale systems powering their GPUs, such as Rubin Ultra."

I believe this deal could be huge for Navitas for three main reasons.

First, the 800V HVDC architecture has the potential to improve power efficiency by 5% and reduce maintenance costs by up to 70%. You probably know better than I do how hungry are the big four hyperscalers for energy. Meta recently closed a 20-year deal with Constellation and Coreweave just signed two 15-year deals with Applied Digital to make use of their facilities and access to 250MW of power. Any drop in power usage is great news for these folks, who just happen to be the main buyers of Nvidia’s current generation of GPUs.

The next reason is the ridiculous demand for AI workloads, which hasn't blinked since DeepSeek caught the market off guard on that beautiful morning on January 27, 2025. Companies like Nebius and Coreweave are expanding their data center footprints, and Palantir's US commercial segment growth is spotless, even after the tariff drama earlier this year. Nvidia's Blackwell GPUs are estimated to generate $98 billion in sales this fiscal year and a further $119 billion in 2026. I expect, at the very least, similar levels of demand for the Vera Rubin family of GPUs when it enters production in late 2026.

Given that the 800V HVDC architecture will be used in the Rubin Ultra, I believe Navitas is well positioned in the mid-term. Why? Because late 2026 is the end of their cash runway. Of course, I am assuming here three things: a constant cash burn, a persistent slowdown in the consumer semiconductor industry, and a small revenue contribution (in the near term) from the collaboration with Nvidia. All these assumptions may be wrong, which is a risk to consider.

Finally, the third factor is hype. People who enjoy wearing suits while typing away at their keyboards would call this investor excitement or sentiment... some may even call it an increase in "market visibility." I like calling things by their name.

Take a look at the volume trend below.

I love comparing the 5D average volume vs the 3-month average value. If we use this metric, Navitas is at the top of the table (by the way, that's the Mag 7 group). Furthermore, if we were to use the number of shares that exchanged hands in the past day, Navitas would be second in that group, behind Nvidia.

There is no doubt that this ticker is popping in the screeners of many investors and speculators, like myself, who don't mind placing a bet on a stock even if the fundamentals are not there yet. Don't get me wrong, in two years, if the collaboration with Nvidia for the 800 V HVDC architecture goes well, I foresee a significant improvement in the company's fundamentals. That said, I am unable to estimate how much as I believe any projection of this kind will be off by several orders of magnitude. One simply cannot predict the exact demand for the Vera Rubin family of GPUs and whether the 800 V HVDC architecture will achieve its projected 5% improvement in power consumption.

Risks

I'll be as clear and direct as I can. The risk for this stock to come back to $2, fast, is significant.

While the collaboration with Nvidia is great news, I don't expect to see a major contribution to revenue any time soon. Until the Rubin Ultra is generally available to the hyperscalers (i.e., late 2026, possibly early 2027), the company is at the mercy of the consumer semiconductor industry.

As shown in the charts above, the decline in revenue is significant due to lower demand for new smartphones (and therefore fast chargers). Furthermore, management noted some weaknesses in the EV and solar markets. These are two industries I’m not especially confident about (even though I own Tesla, but that's another story).

So, long story short, don't expect a massive improvement in fundamentals before the end of 2026.

Conclusion

I rate Navitas as a Strong Buy. My bull case revolves around three pillars.

First, the collaboration with Nvidia has the potential to diversify the revenue stream of the company, moving away from power chips for consumer electronic devices, into high margin AI accelerators.

Second, the current fundamentals are not horrible. They are not good either, but the company is debt free, has a 8 figure quarterly revenue stream from the 10 biggest mobile OEMs, and enough cash to stay afloat until the end of 2026, when the Vera Ultra will (possibly) be generally available for the hyperscalers and AI infrastructure companies.

Finally, there is a component of hype and momentum that I love about this stock. Since the Nvidia announcement, 2 weeks ago, the share price is holding up. Trading volume is among the highest in the U.S. equity market, and the stock is likely capturing the attention of institutional funds with a longer investment timeframe.

Here is a screenshot of my portfolio that holds this stock.

data.title: UTG: The Operating Environment Should Remain Favorable For This CEF
data.description:
Sometimes, the operating environment for a company is more important than what phase of the business cycle the economy is in. Often times, industries go through periods of strength and weakness irrespective of the general economic growth rates, and these more unique cycles have a bigger impact on individual companies or sectors.

An industry that is much better positioned now is the utility sector. These companies benefit in a number of ways from lower rates, and with costs easing as well, the utility industry's operating environment has improved significantly over just the last several years.

One of the best-performing utility funds is the Reaves Utility Income Trust (NYSE:UTG).

The Reaves Utility Income Trust has offered investors total returns of 131.1% over the last 10 years, while the S&P 500 (SPY) has offered investors total returns of 233.6% during the same time period.

Still, UTG has significantly outperformed the S&P 500 over the last year.

The Reaves Utility Income Trust has offered investors total returns of 31.41% over the last year, while the S&P 500 has offered investors total returns of nearly 14% since June 2024. UTG has also noticeably outperformed most peers such as the Utilities Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF (XLU) of recent as well. UTG has offered investors total returns of 30.48% over the last 3 years, while XLU has offered investors total returns of 21.25% during this same time period.

I last wrote about the Reaves Utility Income Trust in August 2024, and I upgraded this fund from a sell to a hold. Today, I'm upgrading this CEF from a hold to a buy. The operating environment has improved significantly for the utilities in the last year with the new and lower rate cycle as well as what likely will be more favorable tax and regulatory reforms with Republican in control of all 3 branches of government. There is still bipartisan support for infrastructure spending in congress, and costs continue to come down as well. UTG's leveraged approach is also uniquely setup to take advantage of what is likely to remain a lower rate environment for some time.

The Reaves Utility Income Trust has an expense ratio of 2.23%, a forward yield of 6.56%, and a $3.05 billion in assets under management. The expense ratio includes the cost of leverage, which has a fixed and floating rate financing components. This CEF is invested 61.66% in utilities, 14.11% in communication, 11.09% in energy, 7.09% in real estate, and 6.05% in the industrials.

UTG is invested nearly 110% in US equities, 5.86% in German equities, 5.24% in Canadian equities, 2.01% in Italian equities, and 0.51% in Norwegian equities.

The fund's top 4 holdings are Talen Energy Corporation (TLN), Deutsche Telekom AG (OTCQX:DTEGY), Entergy Corp. (ETR), and Vistra Corp. (VST). These positions make up nearly 27% of the CEF's combined holdings. UTG has 62 holdings. This fund has an effective leverage rate of 17.51%, or $650 million.

The outlook for the utility industry is positive in 2025, with analysts at firms such as Morningstar issuing bullish reports on this sector. Inflation rates remain low for the Fed to continue cutting rates, with no sign that President Trump's tariffs have caused any meaningful price increases. The President and the Republicans in charge of both Congress and the White House also remain committed to deregulation and lower taxes, both policies that should also benefit the utilities. The Republicans are expected to make Trump's 2017 tax reform bill permanent, which included a reduction in the corporate tax rate to 21%.

The AI build-out helped fuel a good year for the utility industry in 2024, and some analysts are expecting electricity use for this technology at data centers to nearly double by 2032. There is still more public spending expected in the infrastructure sector moving forward as well, with money still needing to be spent from the Inflation Reduction Act. Both parties in Congress are also committed to infrastructure spending.

UTG continues to offer investors both consistent income and solid overall returns. The CEF's distributions have tax advantages as well.

The Reaves Utility Income Trust payouts usually have tax advantages. Over the last fiscal year, UTS' dividends were taxed as 13.66% net income, 1.42% short-term capital gain, 61.66% long-term capital gains, and 23.26% return on capital. The CEF's payouts are reported on an IRS 1099 form.

The economic and market environment is very favorable right now for this Reaves fund, this CEF offers a number of tax advantages other income-based investments do not. While the Reaves Utility Income Trust won't outperform the broader indexes over the long-term, most investors seeking income are more focused on consistent and substantive income than maximizing total returns. UTG should continue to be well positioned to continue to deliver steady payouts with tax advantages.

data.title: Looking For A Signal: Opportunities And Risks For The Telecom Sector
data.description:
Canada’s telecom stocks have been under pressure, but are they potentially poised to make a comeback? MoneyTalk discusses with Vince Valentini, Managing Director, Equity Research, TD Cowen.

Transcript

Anthony Okolie: Well, Canada's telecom stocks have been under pressure. But are there any trends which could help spark a turnaround in the sector? Joining us now to discuss is Vince Valentini, Managing Director for Equity Research at TD Cowen.

And Vince, thanks very much for joining us on the show.

Vince Valentini: Thanks for having me, Anthony.

Anthony Okolie: All right, let's just--

Vince Valentini: I'm always scared that the sector is getting so beaten up that you will stop inviting me, but it's great to be here.

Anthony Okolie: No, we always love having you on the show. All right, so we just got through earnings season. What are some of the big trends or big takeaways that you're seeing there?

Vince Valentini: I'm going to give you two trends. One would be related to the fundamentals of the core wireless business. The other would just be the tone from all these management teams.

Let me start with that-- because it is, I think, a bit of a turning point, I hope, for how these stocks behave over the next year versus the past two years, is there is a lot more humility and acceptance, if I can use those two words from all of the management teams.

These companies, especially the big three large cap names-- BCE (BCE), Telus (TU), and Rogers (RCI)-- have kind of let themselves get off track a little bit over the last few years. Their debt leverage got way too high. Their free cash flow, in some cases, wasn't covering their dividends. And I think investors were just getting fed up with it. So, you can't magically change the growth rate in your core wireless business. There's macrodynamics and population dynamics and price competition that will take some time to work itself out.

But with these management teams can do is control the controllables. And that's what we learned a lot about, both in the Q1 earnings season and also at our conference May 14, which we host every year. A lot of good detail from management about here's what we're doing to fix the problems, whether it's dividend cut from BCE-- which was actually well-received because it now sets them on a stronger foundation-- selling towers, selling some of their health care business, in the case of Telus, to get their debt leverage down, Rogers being a little bit more open about what the plan might be to monetize their sports teams, which is a huge pocket of potential cash to help their balance sheet and, as well, surface value for shareholders.

So Anthony, yeah, I think the biggest takeaway-- and we'll probably dig down into some more of those points in a second. But the biggest deal I think is these companies being a bit more transparent about what their plans are and starting to execute on some of those plans to build a better balance sheet and a financial foundation.

The other takeaway is not quite as rosy, but it's not really unexpected. We knew that the first quarter results were going to be a bit messy in wireless. We saw material declines year over year in subscriber growth, largely related to the new immigration policies in Canada which started to take hold in the second half of last year. But this was the first quarter where we really saw a really tough comparison when there was still very high levels of immigration in Q1 last year, and that went to close to zero in the first quarter of this year. So big drop in sub adds.

No really worse than expected, but still a tough hill for these companies to climb. And continuing a bit of challenge in terms of pricing. I think the pricing dynamic is stabilizing, but even price changes and promotional discounting that would have happened a year ago is still gradually filtering through to the reported numbers. So Q1, from a wireless fundamental perspective, was a bit challenging. A bit of humility on the balance sheet side.

We can turn our attention to Q2 in a follow-up question and talk a bit more detail about it, but I'd just say briefly that the signals we're getting from the companies-- and we're now obviously just over two months into the second quarter-- are that the second quarter is a little bit better than the first quarter, not quite as precipitous a drop in subscriber growth. It's still down year over year, but not to the same sort of 60% magnitude we saw in the first quarter.

And pricing has remained reasonably disciplined throughout the first two months of the period. So, touchwood there. There may be a bit of light at the end of the tunnel on the core fundamentals side, but I think most of the investors are more focused on the balance sheet stuff short term to at least create a better foundation. And then, when the fundamentals start to turn and there's better growth, you've got-- companies will all be better positioned to take advantage of it.

Anthony Okolie: Alright. That's a great overview of the first quarter. Let's dig into some of the themes on your radar, starting with repricing of wireless subscribers. What are you seeing play out there?

Vince Valentini: So, we've done some work recently and published on it of trying to put some math around what percentage of the subscribers in Canada have already repriced. So we've basically been in a price war for two years. The big deal, where Rogers acquired Shaw, and Shaw then had to spin off its wireless business to Québecor (OTCPK:QBCRF, OTCPK:QBCAF) -- so Québecor has been running Freedom Mobile ever since. That happened in April of 2023.

So we've lapped two years on that, and a price war pretty much broke out within a month after that happening. And it wasn't all Québecor's fault as a new entrant. It was sort of preemptive retaliatory behavior by Rogers, Bell, and Telus.

So what we've tried to do is predict-- if we look at how many subscribers have been added over that two-year period relative to the base of current customers, and we can try to triangulate how much of this reprice pain is already behind us. Our math suggests we're in the range of 60-65% have already repriced, which is good news. So, most of the pain is behind us. We think there's probably another 6 to 12 months of the lingering effects of the price war to come.

There's debate in the industry amongst analysts and amongst some of the management teams as to what percentage of total wireless customers in Canada will take advantage of lower prices. The fact is, if you go out and shop around, if you're paying $60 or $70 on wireless, you can probably find an equivalent plan these days in the $40-50 range quite easily. And if you want to move to a flanker brand and maybe take a slightly lesser network quality, you can get yourself down to $30-35 at times.

So, what percentage of people will actually do that versus those who may just be complacent? Some management teams think we're almost there, that only 70% will reprice, and 65% already have-- so, which means you only have one quarter left of pain, and then things will start to turn up in terms of wireless pricing and that key RPU metric that drives wireless service revenue growth.

Some people-- myself-- think it's probably more in the 80-90% range, which means we probably have closer to another year of pain to go through. But however you slice it, whether it's one quarter or three or four quarters, we're definitely closer to the finish line than the beginning of this painful process.

And once everybody's repriced and everybody's happy with their plan, things would start to stabilize. We still have to worry about when will population growth and immigration come back so that subscriber growth can reaccelerate a little bit. That would be nice. Nobody's seeing that or predicting that just yet.

But the price component of service revenue, I think we are getting closer to the end. And some analysts are even getting optimistic that 2026 will be a pretty big inflection up. We haven't quite made that call yet. We want to see a little bit more evidence. But, yes, the percentage of customers that have repriced is becoming critical math with investors right now to know how much longer we have to endure this painful, slow revenue growth environment.

Anthony Okolie: Now, nothing that you highlight in your reporting is-- you identify non-core asset sales as a theme. What is driving this trend?

Vince Valentini: So, back to the humility and acceptance earlier, Anthony, is it is everybody realizes their debt leverage got too high. Everybody got there for different reasons. For Bell and Telus, they were spending billions of dollars on capital expenditures to upgrade their networks to fiber over the course of 10-15 years. Plus, they did some acquisitions. For Rogers, it's really all about the Shaw acquisition and funding that $26 billion deal mostly with debt.

But whatever way they got there, they all reached the same spot where their debt leverage is nearing four times debt to EBITDA. And in the case of Rogers, it got even higher than that temporarily after they acquired Shaw.

And people just aren't comfortable with that. Interest rates aren't at the historic lows they were five years ago. You do have more competition, which has slowed down your cash flow growth. So I think investors have said, look-- investors and credit rating agencies have said, four times is just too high. You need to get yourself back into close to three times debt leverage. And there'll be a lot more comfort that there's that solid foundation there that then you can afford your future CapEx. You can afford your future dividends. You can afford-- if another tuck-in acquisition comes up, you can do it from a position of strength.

So, that feeds into the non-core asset sales, as that is one of the biggest ways these companies can address that problem is to say, look, we have built up all kinds of things over the years that we don't-- in some cases, we don't need to own at all or, in some cases, we don't need to own 100%.

So, in the case of Rogers, they sold-- they're in the process of finishing a deal to sell off 49% of their wireless backhaul infrastructure to Blackstone (BX) for $7 billion-- it's probably the biggest deal in the space. A bunch of them-- BCE and Rogers both have sports teams that they can monetize, which don't really generate any cash flow. And those are on the list.

I mentioned earlier, Telus has identified their cell towers as something they may partially monetize. They also own a very big health care IT business, which they're not going to sell the whole thing, it's core to their growth strategy. But they don't necessarily need to own 100%. So they could bring in a minority partner and monetize some of that.

So, we've actually published that there are 13 different-- I won't go through them all here, it would take up our whole half hour-- but 13 different potential non-core asset classes across Rogers, BCE, and Telus that could get monetized. And they're all in the process right now of trying to pick and choose what is the best to monetize. And I'm sure they're out negotiating as we speak to try to see where they can get the best deals.

But that's going to be a very key thing that's already started and will continue throughout this year. And investors very much want to see a finish to that process to see who can execute the best on these deals, get that debt leverage down to three times versus four times. And then we can be much happier about the backdrop for these stocks going forward.

data.title: Annaly Capital: Cheap While Beating Earnings And Hiking Dividends
data.description:
Annaly Capital (NYSE:NLY) is a stock I have covered on again and off again over the years, mostly with pretty lukewarm coverage. The company’s high apparent dividend yield (15%) is a major draw, but on the other hand, the company has cut its payout many times in the past. Largely because of the spotty dividend history, I typically rated Annaly a hold when I covered it in the past. Although I noted in these articles that U.S. homeowners were in a pretty good place at the time, I thought the high use of leverage in Annaly’s funds created major risks to holders. Accordingly, I thought that there was a serious risk of Annaly doing more dividend cuts in the future.

How do I feel about all this today?

Quite differently, to put it mildly. In my most recent Annaly Capital article, I rated the stock a buy, following two hold ratings. Since I last covered Annaly stock, I have continued thinking about it, and my thoughts about the stock have continued to develop.

The situation for Annaly Capital has changed since I last wrote about it. For one thing, Annaly’s stock price is down 5% since January 29, which means that investors who buy now may get a better entry price than that which they’d have gotten if they’d bought in January. For another thing, Annaly recently put out an earnings release that narrowly beat earnings estimates. Finally, the company hiked its dividend. All of the facts just mentioned are material ones that impact the thesis for investing in NLY at today’s price. Accordingly, I am maintaining my Annaly Capital rating at buy. In the ensuing paragraphs I will explain why.

Competitive Position

One thing that was lacking in all my past Annaly Capital articles was analyses of the company’s competitive position. While it’s tempting to think of Mortgage REITs as simply investment portfolios, the fact is that they do have operations and do compete with other mortgage REITs. So, let’s explore Annaly Capital’s competitive position, starting with an overview of competition in the mortgage REIT industry, then moving on to Annaly’s specific strengths and weaknesses.

Mortgage REITs operate in a dynamic competitive environment. They compete with one another, mainly by bidding for the same mortgage backed securities. The more mortgage REITs in the industry, the lower the yield on MBS, as investments increase the “demand” side of the supply and demand equation. There are a few strategies mortgage REITs can use to make themselves more competitive than their peers:

  1. They can opt to avoid new investments in times when yields are too low to be worth the investment.

  2. They can engage in hedging strategies to ensure longer survival than their peers.

  3. They can differentiate themselves as specializing primarily in agency MBS (lower risk) or non-agency MBS (higher yields).

  4. They can market themselves more effectively than their peers, leading to better fundraising results.

Now, let’s look at Annaly’s specific competitive edges. The company appears to have a few of these, as it has slightly outperformed the S&P 500 on a total return basis at times since its IPO (see image below).

How has Annaly achieved this outperformance?

First, it specializes mainly in agency MBS, which helps with the “survival” aspect of mortgage REIT competition. Agency MBS are fully insured by their issuers, Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, which makes them less risky than non-agency MBS, which usually aren’t insured. Annaly does have some non-agency MBS in its portfolio, but it is not big as a percentage of the whole. This makes Annaly less risky than some of its competitors.

Second, Annaly has a sophisticated hedging strategy that keeps it from suffering the worst outcomes that are sometimes observed in the mortgage REIT industry. The company hedges against credit risk, interest rate risk, and other risk factors. These robust hedging strategies keep the company pretty safe, as can be seen in the fact that it survived the 2008 financial crisis, when even Fannie Mae and Freddy Mac had to be taken into government conservatorship.

Third and finally, Annaly has a high amount of balance sheet leverage, with $88 billion in debt to $13 billion in equity. This leverage boosts the company's yield.

In the past, I considered NLY's debt a major risk factor, but today I’m not as sure about that. Annaly’s hedging strategy allows it to be pretty safe even with a large amount of balance sheet debt. And, as I will show in the next section, the U.S. homeowner is quite strong right now, which means that there is not an extreme amount of risk to the company’s portfolio.

The Historically Strong U.S. Homeowner

A big advantage Annaly and other Mortgage REITs have right now is a strong U.S. homeowner. Although most of NLY’s MBS are insured, a portion are not, so credit risk is a real risk for the REIT. The strength of the U.S. homeowner at the moment argues that this risk will not ruin Annaly Capital.

First, let’s look at affordability. The median U.S. home sale price is $416,000, while the average U.S. homeowner can afford a $447,000 home. These estimates come from the St. Louis Fed and Redfin, respectively. Such estimates from reputable organizations suggest that the average U.S. homeowner can afford what they own.

Next, we can look at credit metrics. The median home price to income ratio is 4.6, which is considerably lower than at the peak of the 2007/2008 housing bubble. It is higher than it was for most of the 2010s, however.

Household debt is currently 61.7% of GDP, which is fairly low by historical standards.

Last but not least, the average U.S. mortgage rate is 6.89%, which is high by the standards of the last decade, but down from the 2022 high.

All of these stats combine argue that the U.S. homeowner is fairly strong right now, which means that the risk of mortgage defaults is not too severe. That’s good for Annaly.

Performance

Next, let’s look at how Annaly Capital has performed in recent years.

Annaly Capital earned $0.72 in EPS in its most recent quarter. The figure increased by 12.5% year over year. The company’s interest income surged 20% in the period, but interest expense grew even more, resulting in negative net interest income (NII). Despite the negative NII, management hiked the dividend after Q1 earnings came out, suggesting they think the company has good prospects.

Annaly Capital’s profitability is tricky to assess. The company has a sky-high 95% gross profit margin, which is good (though not atypical of mortgage REITs, which have little in the way of selling costs). However, the company’s NII collapse in the first quarter dragged down TTM performance, to the point where the company’s margins are lower than they normally are. It's better to look at long term averages here. To that end, MacroTrends has a chart that shows that NLY’s long term average net margin is usually in the double digits. So, it is normally a profitable REIT.

Finally, we can look at dividend performance. As mentioned previously, NLY recently reversed its trend of dividend cuts, by hiking its dividend in the first quarter. As a result of this, the stock now has a 15% yield. The REIT’s payout ratio is 95%, which is high but not unusually so by REIT standards. If rates come down then Annaly should be able to keep paying its dividend.

Valuation

Last but not least, let’s turn to valuation.

Annaly Capital is a pretty cheap stock going by multiples, trading at 6.7 times adjusted earnings, 0.98 times book value and 7.8 times operating cash flow. These multiples are lower than those of Annaly’s sector, which may indicate undervaluation. You can see more multiples in the image below:

Second, we can do a discounted dividend analysis based on Annaly’s dividend. Assuming that the company can keep its dividend at $0.70 per quarter, then the annual payout is $2.80 per year. If you discount $2.80 at a 10% discount rate–a rate that incorporates considerable risk–then you get a $28 fair value estimate. Annaly Capital trades at just $19 today. So it appears worth the investment even in a no-growth scenario. If interest rates start falling, this REIT could really fly.

The Bottom Line

The bottom line on Annaly Capital is that it is a high yielder that appears likely to keep the high income coming well into the future. Cheap, well hedged and backed by a stellar long term track record, it could perform well–especially in a falling rate scenario.

Market Overview

The first quarter ushered in a change of US leadership and with it a change in global equity market dynamics.

Though 2025 started well for risk assets broadly, sour sentiment took hold of US markets in mid-February as concerns mounted about the economic impacts of the Trump administration’s policy priorities. Meanwhile, non-US markets appeared to be reinvigorated by plans for greater fiscal spending in Germany and China. In contrast with recent US dominance, the MSCI EAFE Index outperformed the S&P 500 Index handily during the first quarter, 6.9% to -4.3%, while US exposure drove a 1.8% decline in the MSCI World Index. In another trend reversal, value outperformed growth across markets.1

Of course, circumstances changed dramatically in the early days of the second quarter, as we will discuss later in this report.

Sentiment Journey

Trump’s election victory in November 2024 initially fueled another leg higher in equities, as markets hoped the campaign trail promises of pro-growth deregulation and stimulative tax policy would bolster earnings for a range of US companies amid steady economic growth and cooling inflation. This optimism began to fade well before Trump took office in late January, however, and the unraveling of the so-called “Trump trade” was underway in earnest by mid-February as it became clear that the new administration’s policy timeline prioritized tariffs and program cuts over stimulative measures. It wasn’t long before domestic stock markets had given back all of their post-election day gains and then some.2

Non-US markets were mostly unfazed, however, buoyed by the prospect of increased fiscal spending. Germany’s new governing coalition spearheaded legislation to permanently exempt defense spending above 1% of GDP from its restrictive constitutional debt brake and created a €500 billion infrastructure fund, allowing the European country with the most fiscal space the flexibility to deploy it.3 Earlier, the annual meeting of China’s parliament emphasized the need to boost consumption in the face of external threats like the escalating trade war with the US.4 This comes on top of plans to increase fiscal support by 1 to 2% of GDP this year.

In the US, hard economic data released in the first quarter continued to show persistent economic growth alongside stubbornly above-target inflation in the US. However, soft indicators appeared to capture the early negative impacts of the new administration’s policy erraticism and aggressive cost cutting. Consumers, for one, have grown decidedly more cautious in the face of the prevailing uncertainty. Consumer confidence as measured by The Conference Board has declined for four consecutive months, and its expectations index—which captures consumers’ short-term outlook for income, business and labor-market conditions—fell to a 12-year low in March and sits well below the threshold that usually signals coming recession.5 A survey from the University of Michigan indicated similarly crumbling sentiment,6 while the New York Fed’s latest consumer data revealed notable pessimism across prospects for household finances, employment, loan delinquencies and credit access.7

Business attitudes have also darkened. On Main Street USA, the NFIB Small Business Optimism Index depicted waning confidence in the economy and high and rising uncertainty in what the future holds.8 Globally, business confidence in the year ahead approached post-Covid lows in March, with US manufacturing Purchasing Managers’ Index (PMI) slipping to below 50 indicating expectations of contracting activity.9 Perhaps most tellingly, tariffs were mentioned more than 800 times in investor events or conference calls during the first quarter, the highest rate in 15 years.10 With this level of concern evident even before the announcement of Trump’s tariff plan, it seems likely the topic will dominate earnings calls in the weeks ahead.

Mind the Gap

While the environment has changed dramatically in the early days of the second quarter—more on that later—it’s worth noting that the US economy entered the year with an ongoing positive output gap, as well as below-average unemployment and above-average wage growth. A positive output gap indicates the economy is operating above potential, and the resulting inflationary impulse typically invites a cooling response from policymakers. For example, the Federal Reserve’s rate hikes in 2022 and 2023 in response to persistently high inflation narrowed but did not close the output gap. With monetary policy settings currently pretty close to neutral, the persistence of the positive output gap likely can be attributed to expansionary fiscal policy and ongoing deficit spending.11

Persistent deficits have also helped support corporate earnings, in our view, and skewed risk perception in US markets. From World War II through the global financial crisis, the country’s primary fiscal position was more or less in balance through decades of offsetting deficits and surpluses. Since 2009, however, economic cycles have been characterized by deeper primary deficits at the bottom and shallower deficits at the top.12 The level of S&P 500 earnings has more or less kept pace with the mounting pile of debt that has resulted from persistent deficit spending for much of the twenty-first century.13

Which is to say that as government debt goes, so too goes the nominal drift in the economy and corporate participation in it. Assuming that current levels of debt accumulation are needed to support expectations of continued earnings growth, a reacceleration in payroll growth, wage growth and inflation are plausible—though underappreciated—byproducts.

Between a Rock and a Hard Place

While it’s painless to acknowledge the structural deterioration of the country’s fiscal profile, fixing it is not. Easy fiscal policy begets difficult choices: Do nothing to contain spending and increase the risk of inflation, or take action to curb it and increase the risk of recession.

Federal outlays as a percentage of GDP have been chronically high since the onset of Covid-19 and amounted to around 23% in fiscal 2024.14 Dividing federal spending into its three major components—discretionary, net interest and mandatory—can help illuminate the challenges facing policymakers seeking to address the issue.

Discretionary spending comprises nondefense and defense spending and is funded annually through the congressional appropriations process. Nondefense discretionary spending supports a wide range of programs, from education and public health to scientific research and tax collections. While the Trump administration has identified nondefense discretionary spending as the lowest-hanging and most politically expedient fruit to pick, at 3.3% of GDP, it’s a small harvest. So while the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) makes headlines by taking a chainsaw to these outlays, its ability to impact the deficit and debt is minimal. For example, while a 20% cut to the nondefense discretionary budget would be significant to the programs impacted, it would only amount to about 0.6% of GDP.

On the defense side, spending has shifted markedly lower since the end of the Cold War and in 2024 comprised just 2.9% of GDP, slightly above the all-time low of 2.7% in 1999. Given the shifting alliances amid an increasingly shaky geopolitical landscape, it’s hard to imagine this rate going lower. The Trump administration has urged its allies in the North Atlantic Treaty Organization to increase their defense spending in the face of new and emerging threats, and Secretary of State Rubio has signaled that the US would follow suit.15 Trump himself recently announced that he plans to request $1 trillion for defense in the fiscal 2026 budget, up from about $850 billion in 2025.16

Assuming a unilateral restructuring or voluntary default of US debt is off the table, net interest spending is untouchable. The cost of servicing government debt has climbed rapidly in recent years as sub-2% debt issued during the Covid-19 period matured and was replaced by debt at rates in excess of 4%; this trend is forecast to continue.17 Notably, 2024 represented the first year that net interest spending exceeded defense spending. “Ferguson’s Law” posits that any power that spends more on debt servicing than on defense faces a heightened risk of imperial decline. With the US in violation of this law, there is perhaps some inevitability to the country’s pullback from the global stage.18

With both discretionary and net interest spending unlikely sources of relief, meaningful progress on US debt likely will require entitlement reform and/or higher revenue. The bulk of government outlays— representing 14.1% of GDP in 2024—are directed toward programs like Social Security, Medicare and Medicaid, mandatory commitments whose parameters are enshrined into law. Mustering the necessary political will for changes to programs so broadly popular with voters seems like an insurmountable challenge. Generating additional revenue through higher taxes is also appears difficult to reach with Republicans controlling the White House and both chambers of Congress.

Enter tariffs. Charged to importers at the port of entry into the US and mostly passed along to end consumers, tariffs act as a sort of backdoor consumption tax on Americans. Yes, they generate revenue for the federal government, but at the likely cost of higher prices and slower economic activity.

Trump championed the benefits of tariffs throughout his presidential campaign, and he quickly—if with shifting degrees of conviction— slapped new levies on specific countries (notably, Canada, Mexico and China) and industries (steel and aluminum) upon taking office.

Designating April 2 as “Liberation Day,” Trump appeared in the White House’s Rose Garden to introduce a wide-ranging package of tariffs, imposing a baseline 10% charge on all imports globally and steeper rates—referred to as, but not actually, “reciprocal”—on countries deemed to be “bad actors.”19

More extreme than markets seemed to anticipate, the tariff announcement unleashed a rout across risk assets worldwide and a significant spike in volatility. Perhaps of greater concern was the selloff in Treasuries and the US dollar, which may suggest wavering conviction in these assets as reliable “safe havens” during periods of unrest. A week later, via social media and with decidedly less fanfare, Trump announced that while the 10% baseline tariff would continue, the “reciprocal” tariff would be paused for all countries except China, which is now subject to a tariff of 145%.20

Seeking Ballast amid the Chaos

We make a point of acknowledging our inability to forecast the future even in the most placid of times, but Trumps’s to and fro approach to trade policy—which thus far has included tariffs, counter-tariffs, counter-counter-tariffs, tariff pauses, etc.—has introduced profound instability to the global order.

Given the lack of both historical comparisons and clarity around many important variables, directionality may be the best one can hope for in a forecast. To that end, the global tariff framework as currently designed likely would raise prices and slow economic activity in the US while weighing on both prices and economic growth abroad. Further, tariffs and their indeterminate impact reflect another investment risk in a world rife with them, the aggregate impact of which suggests a greater likelihood of nonlinear market moves such as those we have seen in recent days.

But rather than making concentrated bets on the direction of markets, we continue to focus on investing in a diversified basket of individual assets that we believe have the potential to demonstrate resilience across multiple states of the world. In certain portfolios, this includes gold, whose resilience year-to-date underscores why we advocate for a strategic exposure to the metal as a potential hedge against adverse market outcomes. Gold also serves as a source of long-term deferred purchasing power, easily convertible to cash when market dislocations produce opportunities to invest in high-quality businesses at prices that have suddenly become much more attractive.

Portfolio Review

U.S. Fund A Shares (without sales charge*) posted a return of 4.06% in first quarter 2025. Materials, consumer staples and health care were the leading contributors among equity sectors, while information technology, communication services and consumer discretionary were the largest detractors. The US Value Fund outperformed the S&P 500 Index in the period.

Leading contributors in the First Eagle U.S. Fund this quarter included gold bullion, Philip Morris International Inc. (PM), Elevance Health, Inc. (ELV), HCA Healthcare Inc. (HCA) and Bank of New York Mellon Corporation (BK).

Gold continued to serve its role as a potential hedge against adverse market conditions during the quarter, setting a series of new nominal highs as the dollar slumped and real interest rates fell. Sustained geopolitical unrest continued to underpin demand from central banks, as did the potential fallout from a shifting and uncertain global trade framework.

Philip Morris reported strong results during the quarter, propelled by both smoke-free and combustible products. With costs denominated in US dollars but approximately two-thirds of revenues sourced from non-US markets, we believe the company should be positioned to benefit from a weaker US dollar. Investors’ recent interest in companies that consistently generate cash may have provided an additional tailwind, along with the reduced emphasis on socially responsible investing. Facilitated by strong operations, management has consistently returned cash to shareholders through dividends and stock repurchases.

Elevance Health is the health insurer and healthcare-services provider formerly known as Anthem. Elevance’s margins were pressured last year by higher Medicaid utilization rates, but they have been recovering alongside rising reimbursement rates, to the benefit of the stock.

HCA Healthcare is the largest for-profit hospital operator in the US. The company reported continued strong growth during the quarter, and operational improvements across key performance indicators that quelled—at least for now—investor fears about significant cuts to entitlement programs such as Medicaid and Medicare. We believe HCA’s difficult-to-replicate network of large urban hospitals provides a measure of insulation against policy-related disruption. We view HCA’s management as an effective steward of both operations and the balance sheet as it continues to return capital to shareholders through share buybacks.

Bank of New York Mellon is the world’s largest custody bank, with a $2.1 trillion investment management business. Results reported during the quarter exceeded investor expectations, reflecting higher investment service fees supported by strong client acquisitions and higher account balances. The stock also benefited from positive sentiment toward financial stocks in general during the quarter.

Management’s ongoing focus on efficiency and expense control has contributed to wider margins to better support debt obligations.

The leading detractors in the quarter were Oracle Corporation (ORCL), Alphabet Inc. Class C (GOOG, GOOGL), Salesforce.com, Inc. (CRM), Alphabet Inc. Class A and Noble Corporation PLC Class A (NE).

Oracle is one of the world’s largest independent enterprise software companies. The company reported lower-than-expected results for its most recent quarter and provided lower-than-expected forward guidance. Oracle cited capacity constraints as a factor in its sales miss but noted that its cloud infrastructure business bookings have increased. Oracle said capital expenditures would be higher than previously expected as it expands its data-center capacity. We believe Oracle should be positioned to capture recurring and sustainable revenue streams by providing services that support the use and adoption of artificial intelligence.

Shares of Alphabet, the parent company of Google and YouTube, traded lower after it reported slower-than-expected sales growth in its cloud business, which provides services that range from basic data storage to artificial intelligence-powered analytical tools. The company attributed this slowdown to capacity constraints and increased its capital expenditure budget in order to build out capacity. We believe that Alphabet’s market position remains strong, and we like the company’s focus on returning cash to shareholders through dividends and stock buybacks.

Shares of enterprise software developer Salesforce traded down after the company reported lower-than-expected results for its most recent quarter and provided lower-than-expected forward guidance. This prompted concerns about its transformation from a service business to an artificial intelligence-powered enterprise business (its Agentforce platform). We continue to like Salesforce’s operational strength, market dominance and focus on balancing growth with improving profitability.

Shares of Alphabet, the parent company of Google and YouTube, traded lower after it reported slower-than-expected sales growth in its cloud business, which provides services that range from basic data storage to artificial intelligence-powered analytical tools. The company attributed this slowdown to capacity constraints and increased its capital expenditure budget in order to build out capacity. We believe that Alphabet’s market position remains strong, and we like the company’s focus on returning cash to shareholders through dividends and stock buybacks.

Noble is a deepwater drilling contractor for the oil and gas industry. The industry continues to experience softness in operator activity, and demand for Noble’s services fell alongside declining OPEC production. We note that industry fundamentals remain relatively healthy, with a modest decline in utilization and rational capacity.

With its high-capacity utilization, attractive contracted day rates and manageable debt, Noble should be positioned to endure the current lull in operator activity. Longer term, we expect deepwater oil exploration to comprise a meaningful component of global supply, to the benefit of scale players like Noble.

We appreciate your confidence and thank you for your support.

data.title: Infineon: Positioned For Recovery, Powered By Autos And China
data.description:
Infineon Technologies (OTCQX:IFNNY) is a leader in discrete power semiconductors with significant exposure to the automotive segment. The company stands to benefit from the ongoing electrification of vehicles and the rising semiconductor content in cars — particularly as electric vehicles continue to gain share over traditional internal combustion engine models.

As I’ve outlined in prior analyses on ON Semiconductor and STMicroelectronics, the automotive chip segment went through a phase of unsustainable growth post-COVID, driven by OEMs overstocking after pandemic-induced shortages. Semiconductor cycles tend to mirror this dynamic — the steeper the ascent, the harder the eventual correction. Now that dealer and OEM inventories are normalizing, it seems like only a matter of time before a new upcycle begins.

Admittedly, President Trump’s new auto tariffs add uncertainty to the timing of this recovery. But based on recent earnings calls from STM, NXPI, and Infineon, there are increasing signs that the worst is behind us. I believe it’s time to start leaning optimistic, even if short-term supply chain disruptions persist.

Moreover, capital rotation away from U.S. equities due to policy volatility — combined with Germany’s potential shift away from its traditional fiscal conservatism — could support valuation multiples for domestic champions like Infineon.

1. Company Segments

Infineon operates through four main business segments, each tied to secular themes like electrification, energy efficiency, and secure connectivity. Together, they form a diversified portfolio that skews heavily toward industrial and automotive applications.

The Automotive (ATV) division makes up about half of revenue. It includes microcontrollers, sensors, and power chips used across ICEs, EVs, and hybrids. Infineon leads the global market in automotive MCUs and is #1 in discrete power for cars. They are also #1 in automotive semiconductors in general.

Volumes are picking up again as inventories normalize. Q2 FY25 revenue was up 6% sequentially. Margins are starting to recover (20.7% vs 19.6% last quarter). FX also helped.

However margins are still below the 25–26% range from last year. Idle costs and annual price resets continue to weigh. Tariffs add risk on the global demand side.

The Power & Sensor Systems (PSS) segment includes discrete power devices and control ICs for consumer and industrial applications. This is also where the AI power story lives. The segment is seeing strength in AI server demand, while industrial and consumer-related end markets continue to drag. PSS became more relevant after Infineon guided for €600 million in AI-related revenue in 2025 and over €1 billion in 2026 — figures that suggest meaningful share gains, particularly at the expense of MPWR (MPWR) in power solutions for NVIDIA platforms.

Green Industrial Power (GIP) is where Infineon plays the energy transition: solar, industrial drives, motor control.

Revenue rebounded 17% QoQ in Q2 FY25 after a weak Q1, with some early signs of industrial activity returning and inventories easing. That said, margins dropped sharply to just 9.6%, about half of where they were late last year. Underutilization and pricing pressure continue to weigh. The recovery is still slow and fragile.

We're also seeing broader shifts in legislation in both the U.S. and Europe, along with specific import tariffs in the U.S., which are putting serious pressure on solar companies — and that’s starting to ripple into the supply chain.

Connected Secure Systems (CSS) is the smallest piece — covering chip cards, identity protection, and IoT security. Revenue was up 3% QoQ, with margin improving to 11.2%. Security ICs and auto MCUs helped. However margins are still far from prior margin levels (15%+). IoT and general security demand remain weak. Macro continues to cap upside.

Overall, Infineon plays in power applications mainly through discrete components — MOSFETs, IGBTs — rather than analog systems. These are single-function devices, generally cheaper and more commoditized, but still critical in high-voltage environments like EVs, renewables, and industrial systems, where efficiency and thermal performance are essential.

By looking at the numbers, we see that overall revenue peaked in 2023 and is now going through a broader correction. Even automotive, which had remained relatively stable, experienced a degree of correction in the first two quarters of fiscal year 2025.

However, Q2 showed sequential growth across all segments compared to Q1. The Q3 outlook — guiding for €3.7 billion in revenue — also points to another step in the recovery. Management’s full-year guidance of slightly lower revenue in FY2025 versus the prior year implies a stronger second half, since the annualized first half would otherwise suggest a decline of more than 5%. These are all signs that the bottom is likely behind us.

2. China Exposure

Infineon held a 13.9% share of the Chinese automotive semiconductor market in 2024, according to its own data. Looking ahead, the company expects to continue gaining share among local OEMs in its market outlook for calendar year 2025.

That matters because while EV volumes in key markets like the U.S. and Japan are expected to decline this year, China remains a bright spot — both for vehicle demand overall and for EV adoption specifically. China accounts for roughly one-third of the global automotive market, and unlike Western regions where incentives are being pulled back or demand is stalling, China’s EV momentum continues to build.

There are structural reasons behind this. EVs help reduce oil import dependency, which is strategically important for China. Daily commuting patterns are typically short-range, meaning range anxiety is less of a limiting factor. And with limited inter-regional mobility compared to countries like the U.S., infrastructure requirements are easier to address. In that context, being the top automotive chip supplier in the country that is likely to lead in EV numbers growth is a clear positive.

Infineon estimates that the semiconductor bill for ICE cars is near $750 per vehicle, while BEVs carry around $1,300. There's also an expectation that by 2030 this number will rise to around $1,650 per vehicle. In 2024, more than 50% of cars sold in China were electric — a share that should continue to grow. This transition from ICE to BEVs should keep driving growth for semiconductor companies exposed to China.

In 2024, only approximately 10% of the total fleet in China was electric, which also leaves room for continued growth as older cars get replaced. Not to mention that the entire car market in China is still expected to grow.

With these numbers in mind, assuming that the EV share can go as high as 80% in 2030, with the overall number of vehicles sold growing 4% per year, and content in ICEs increasing 2% and in BEVs 4% annually, the automotive semiconductor market in China could grow by more than 85% from 2024 to 2030. From there, it becomes a matter of market share and competition.

We’re already seeing the divergence: BYD continues to grow deliveries, while Tesla is cutting prices and struggling to maintain volumes in the U.S. Infineon’s positioning in China gives it exposure not just to volumes, but to EV content growth.

This is helping their topline as the management explained in Q3 FY2024 earnings call.

China sees healthy consumer demand, which helps us particularly given our #1 automotive market position there. Meanwhile, demand in Western markets is tepid.

This has also been demonstrated in their last earnings call:

And just a case in point, as you asked also for the overall effect on Infineon, which, of course, is highly good part is automotive. Our revenue in China for automotive year-over-year grew double digit.

Companies like ON Semiconductor, which are more exposed to the U.S. market — and to Tesla in particular — are feeling the pressure more than Infineon, as demand softness hits harder on their side of the EV chain.

However, this exposure also brings risk — as we’ll discuss later. China has made it clear that it wants greater semiconductor autonomy, and automotive chips are likely to be among the first targets. They’re less complex than edge AI processors or advanced logic, making them more feasible for domestic firms to replicate and scale.

3. The deal with Marvell (MRVL)

Last month, Infineon announced a $2.5 billion deal to acquire Marvell’s automotive Ethernet business.

I believe this acquisition further strengthens Infineon’s offering by complementing its core microcontroller business. It also has the potential to be accretive to gross margins — the acquired segment typically operates at around 60%, compared to Infineon’s current margin profile in the low 40s.

For calendar 2025, the business is expected to generate $225–250 million in revenue. At first glance, a ~10x revenue multiple may seem steep. But it’s important to consider the strategic angle: Infineon already has deep relationships with Chinese automotive OEMs, giving it a much stronger cross-selling platform than Marvell could offer on its own. In Infineon’s hands, this business has a clearer path to scale — and with Ethernet connectivity becoming central to the software-defined vehicle, the timing looks right. I really liked this acquisition.

4. Financial model

Infineon has a long-term operating model that has evolved over time. Back in their 2021 Investor Day, the company was guiding for 9% revenue growth with a segment result margin of 19%. Since then, they’ve signaled increased confidence in their ability to deliver better margins than initially expected. I believe part of that comes from automotive not only dominating the revenue mix, but also contributing an even larger share of the company’s operating profit — which structurally supports higher margins going forward.

Overall, the segment result margin last quarter was around 16.75%. As revenue recovers, operating leverage should kick in — meaning margin expansion can help drive operating income growth at a faster pace than topline growth.

That said, Infineon operates in a cyclical business. While revenue has at times exceeded its older guidance by a wide margin, it's only natural to see some downward oscillation as part of the cycle.

5. Projections

Analysts are projecting a gradual recovery after this year, broadly in line with management’s guidance. In 2025, however, most of the revenue growth in dollar terms comes from FX effects — not actual growth in euros.

The real story is in the EPS consensus. That’s where margin expansion becomes more visible, reinforcing my view that operating leverage will drive EPS growth well above what topline numbers alone suggest.

It’s also worth noting that there are far fewer analysts forecasting EPS than there are for revenue. That gap can introduce distortions when comparing average revenue expectations to average EPS projections.

By running a sensitivity analysis on the expected average revenue (in euros) and applying a 25% EBIT margin, we can get a sense of what kind of assumptions are implicitly baked into the projections.

I believe these margin levels are feasible. Back in FY2023, with even lower revenue levels, Infineon already delivered an EBIT margin of 26.3%.

As discussed, margins are a much stronger driver of EPS than revenue CAGR. That said, it would be surprising to see a scenario where revenue grows but margins stay low — especially in a business with this level of operating leverage.

Below, I show the sensitivity to different revenue CAGRs and margin assumptions by 2027.

6. Valuation

In a relative analysis, Infineon isn’t exactly cheap compared to its peers. However, it likely offers the best combination of growth and valuation in the group. Momentum also seems more favorable — probably driven by the AI revenue story gaining traction and China exposure providing a tailwind to the topline, while others face steeper demand softness.

In terms of forward multiples, I have to admit the market is already pricing in at least part of the recovery we’re seeing. Current forward multiples are about 10% above the company’s historical average as of the day I’m writing this. That doesn’t really bother me, though — I believe there are at least two to three years of growth ahead, and a one-year forward multiple doesn’t fully capture that.

If we assume €2.49 in EPS by 2027 — based on a 23.5% EBIT margin and 10% revenue CAGR — and apply Infineon’s historical multiple of 20x, that would imply a share price of around €50 by the end of fiscal 2026. Discounting that at a conservative 15% rate gives a present value of roughly €45.5. Depending on the exchange rate, this could equate to around $50 for the ADR, still offering upside from current levels.

7. Risks and opportunities

- China exposure is a clear positive in the short term. It remains the fastest-growing automotive market and should drive volume for the next several years. Assuming Infineon maintains share, the expanding TAM could support ~5% CAGR in automotive revenue. That said, it’s also the segment's biggest risk: competition in China is fierce, and if local players gain traction, Infineon’s share — and by extension, its guidance — could come under pressure. Auto OEMs tend to be sticky with suppliers, and qualification cycles are long, but it’s something to watch. The company’s backlog will be a key signal here.

- FX risk adds another layer of volatility. Infineon earns a meaningful share of its revenue in U.S. dollars and other currencies, while much of its cost base is in euros. If the euro strengthens, both revenue (in reporting terms) and margins could be negatively impacted. On the flip side, ADR holders in the U.S. may see the dollar value of their shares rise as the euro appreciates. The net effect depends on Infineon’s hedging strategy. That currency shift, if driven by capital flowing out of the U.S. and into Europe, could also push up valuation multiples — something we’re already starting to see in European markets.

- Capital flows and positioning could continue to benefit Infineon. As investors look to diversify out of U.S. assets — particularly after years of overweight exposure — European equities stand to gain. Infineon, with its scale, liquidity, and thematic exposure, is well-positioned to attract that flow. Additionally, Germany’s shift away from strict fiscal discipline, financed through higher public spending and debt issuance, may reinforce the trend as it creates room for economic stimulus and attracts yield-seeking capital.

- Tariffs also remain a challenge, as they can weigh on demand and increase costs. The supply chain will take time to adjust, and some additional volatility in fundamentals should be expected.

- Multiple compression is another risk. Markets could shift to a risk-off mode again, and as we've seen, Infineon is currently trading above its historical multiples. There's always the possibility that valuations get revised down before earnings have a chance to catch up — especially if macro sentiment turns or guidance disappoints.

8. Conclusion

Infineon is a dominant player in the automotive semiconductor market and is well positioned to benefit from the continued growth of the Chinese EV and auto segments. Beyond that, other business units are currently operating at low levels and are likely to rebound, supporting operating results over the next few years.

As a European-based company, Infineon also offers investors regional diversification outside of the U.S. While valuation multiples are somewhat stretched — and that is a real risk — I’d suggest investors define a target portfolio allocation and build the position gradually to help mitigate timing risk. That same target can also serve as a reference point for trimming or adding to the position over time.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best International Stock Idea investment competition, which runs through June 6. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss.
