data.title: Sagimet: Positive China Partner Acne Vulgaris Data Bodes Well For TVB-3567
data.description:
Sagimet Biosciences Overview

Today Sagimet Biosciences Inc. (NASDAQ:SGMT) and its partner in China Ascletis Bioscience reported positive results from a phase 3 study using Fatty Acid Synthase [FASN] inhibitor denifanstat for the treatment of patients with acne vulgaris. The thing is that this drug is licensed by Ascletis in China [known as AS40] for the treatment of patients with acne. However, Sagimet has rights to develop this drug for the treatment of patients with metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis [MASH] throughout the rest of the globe. What this news does is that it bodes well for the company advancing another FASN inhibitor known as TVB-3567 for the treatment of patients with acne vulgaris.

Speaking of which, it has already initiated a phase 1 study using this next generation version of this drug to target this specific group of patients. The company just became even more valuable today after its Chinese partner Ascletis released data showing that a FASN inhibitor was successful in treating these patients with acne vulgaris. What's even far better than that, is that this biotech has also already been able to establish positive results from its phase 2b FASCINATE-2 study, which explored the use of denifanstat for the treatment of patients with F2-F3 MASH.

While this specific program is phase 3 ready, the company is taking a completely different approach to this. Especially since it doesn't have the funding at the moment to run a large phase 3 MASH trial of this caliber. Instead, it wants to initiate a phase 1b study using denifanstat as an FASN inhibitor in combination with FDA approved MASH drug REZDIFFRA [resmetirom]. The point is to examine the pharmacokinetic [PK] profile of this drug in the soon to be initiated phase 1 study for this combination testing. Turnaround time for a first look into this denifantstat + resmetirom combination is not going to take a long time either because data from this early-stage trial is expected to be released in the 1st half of 2026.

Denifanstat Establishing Sufficient Proof Of Treating Acne Vulgaris Bodes Well For U.S. Development

As I noted above, Sagimet's partner Ascletis reported positive results from a phase 3 study conducted in China to treat patients with Acne Vulgaris. Before going over this late-stage clinical data released by its partner, plus any catalysts to come about from this specific program, it is first important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity for the company could end up being. Acne Vulgaris is a type of skin disorder whereby skin inflammation is present and causes the formation of pustules, papules and nodules on the face in a chronic fashion. Although, the premise is that this disorder forms on the face of a patient, it is also quite possible that it could form on other parts of a person's body such as the Trunk area, back and arms.

The inflammation is primarily present in the pilosebaceous unit of the patient. What the heck is this pilosebaceous unit? It is specifically comprised of the hair follicle, sebaceous gland and the arrector pili muscle. One last item to point out is that acne doesn't necessarily have to present itself with inflammation. It can also be non-inflammatory as well, but still these patients need drugs to clear up the pustules and nodules that may form as a result of it.

The global acne treatment market size is projected to reach $17.48 billion by 2032. This is a very large market opportunity indeed, but it is important to highlight that it is specifically targeting patients who have moderate-to-severe disease. As the company notes in its press release, over 50 million people in the United States suffer from acne. Thus, the market opportunity for Sagimet is there, it just needs to deliver on the development of its U.S. FASN inhibitor candidate TVB-3567 in a phase 1 study targeting these acne patients.

In order to see whether or not denifanstat, as an oral FASN inhibitor is going to be able to help treat these patients with moderate-to-severe acne vulgaris, its partner Ascletis ran a study in China treating these patients. That is, this company ran a phase 3 randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial in this specific territory. A total of 480 patients were randomized 1:1 into two specific treatment arms as follows:

  • 50 mg of denifanstat
  • Placebo.

Patients received either of these oral doses once-daily for a total of 12 weeks and then were evaluated for safety/efficacy. The trial being able to meet the primary efficacy endpoints was not an easy feat at all. What makes me say that? Well, this particular phase 3 study had three primary endpoints deployed and they were:

  • Investigator's Global Assessment [IGA] % at Week 12 compared to that of placebo : 33.2% denifanstat compared to 14.6% placebo
  • Percentage change in total lesion count at Week 12 compared to that of placebo - 57.4% denifanstat compared to -35.4% placebo
  • Percentage change in inflammatory lesion count at Week 12 compared to that of placebo : -63.5% denifanstat compared to -43.2% placebo.

All three of these primary efficacy endpoints were met with a statistically significant p-value of p<0.0001. An oral drug fits in well here for the treatment of these patients with acne and this is because believe it or not but not even topical treatments are adhered to. With this being a chronic disorder, patients must continue on treatment. This goes to something else noted in the press release shown today, which is that people don't follow through in taking topical therapies for this disease as opposed to oral therapies. Also, in that about 30% to 40% of patients don't adhere to their topical regimens prescribed. This leaves an open door for an FASN inhibitor like denifanstat to help these patients with a novel mechanism of action [MOA].

With that being said, these patients desperately needed something new as a treatment option. Consider that in over 40 years there has been limited innovation in this acne treatment space. Acletis expects to submit a regulatory application of denifanstat for approval to the China National Medical Products Administration [NMPA], but it is not clear exactly when this will happen as of yet.

What happens with this disorder is that sebocytes, epithelial cells found in sebaceous glands overproduce sebum, causing acne. FASN is the last step of the de novo lipogenesis [DNL] pathway for these sebocytes where overproduction of sebum occurs. This DNL pathway is not ideal, because it is where a majority [About >80% of key sebum lipids such as palmitate and sapienic are produced. Regardless, the point is that FASN inhibition has a huge direct effect on reducing this overproduction of sebum and thus clearing up acne. This is what has been proven in the phase 3 study done by the company's partner Ascletis in China.

The beauty of this biotech is that denifanstat can also be used to target both patients with MASH and patients with cancer. In terms of MASH, the DNL pathway targets hepatocytes of the liver causing a stoppage of steatosis of the liver. Plus, an FASN inhibitor pathway blockage has an inhibitory effect of inflammation [like in acne]. With respect to denifanstat as an FASN inhibitor in helping to treat cancer patients, it blocks what cancer cells need with this mode of action. In cancer, FASN helps to achieve multiple items like helping cancer develop drug resistance, proliferation [expansion of cancer cells] and tumor survival all through intracellular signaling.

Thus, the point here is that the scope of FASN inhibition goes beyond the scope of only being a possible effective treatment option for the treatment of patients with moderate-to-severe acne vulgaris.

SGMT Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Sagimet Biosciences had cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities of $144.6 million as of March 31st of 2025. The company currently doesn't denote how much its cash on hand will last, just that in this SEC Filing it states that it will continue to incur losses and will be required to raise capital through several types of financial instruments. In its Q4 earnings press release, it stated it cash on hand is:

expected to fund operations for at least the next 12 months from the issuance of the financial statements for the year ended December 31, 2024.

With an $18.2 million cash burn in Q1, that implies a current cash runway of about eight quarters, but the burn rate has been increasing.
With that being said, one important thing to be aware of is that it entered into an at-the-market [ATM] offering agreement with Cantor Fitzgerald & Co. [Cantor] back in August of 2024. This specific offer was enacted so that the company could from time to time sell at its own discretion up to $75 million shares of its Series A common stock through Cantor acting as its sales agent.

The good news here is that no sales were done under this ATM for the three months ending March 31st of 2025. However, considering that positive data was just released from its China partner Ascletis today from the phase 3 study, it would not be a surprise to see it possibly use the ATM today. There is no way of knowing whether or not this ATM is used, though until the release of Sagimet's next financial earnings report. What it could also possibly do, maybe in after-hours if needed, is to raise cash by some other means. Either way, it is possible that the management of this company might choose to raise funds a lot earlier than expected.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Sagimet Biosciences. The first risk to consider would be with respect to the positive results released from the phase 3 study done in China, using FASN inhibitor denifanstat for the treatment of patients with moderate-to-severe acne vulgaris. The company is developing another FASN inhibitor from its pipeline, known as TVB-3567, for the treatment of patients with acne. The risk here is that even though denifanstat performed well in several studies [like the most recent phase 3 China study], there is no assurance that this other drug being developed is going to achieve similar or superior results to that which has been shown to date with denifanstat.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the positive results achieved in the phase 2b FASCINATE-2 study, using denifanstat for the treatment of patients with F2/F3 MASH. This program is solid, but a path forward it wants to take is to evaluate the combination of denifanstat as an FASN inhibitor and REZDIFFRA [resmetirom] to treat such patients with MASH in a phase 1 study. A trial such as this is expected to be initiated in the 2nd half of 2025, with a clinical data readout to occur in the 1st half of 2026. The risk here is that there is no assurance that the PK data to be released at that time will be shown to be positive for starters. Nor that the entire clinical data set to be produced will be enough to move forward towards the next stage of clinical testing thereafter.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the financial position that Sagimet Biosciences finds itself in. As I have highlighted above in the "Financials" subsection, it has a going concern for cash. Plus, it has an ATM in place that it had established with Cantor to sell up to $75 million worth of shares of its Series A common stock.

The risk here is that if the ATM is enacted, then it would be a heavy dilution burden for investors of this company. Plus, if another type of financial transaction is ultimately done to raise cash, then it could have a negative impact on its stock price. Either way, this financial risk is something to be aware of, despite the fact that its partner reported positive results from its phase 3 study using denifanstat for the treatment of patients with moderate-to-severe acne vulgaris.

Conclusion

Sagimet Biosciences and its partner in China, Ascletis have done well to develop the use of an oral FASN inhibitor for the treatment of patients with moderate-to-severe acne vulgaris. This was proven by the fact that all three primary efficacy endpoint measures of IGA %, total lesion count % and inflammatory lesion count % at Week 12 were all met with statistical significance in favor of denifanstat. Plus, depending upon what happens in the phase 1 combination study data outcome of putting denifanstat together with resmetirom, then the targeting of MASH could also be possible.

It is expected that the global non-alcoholic steatohepatitis market could reach $39.3 billion by 2031. With positive data being established already in the phase 2b FASCINATE-2 trial, plus the mechanism of action [MOA] of reducing steatosis and inflammation in place, then it's possible that the addition of denifanstat to an already approved FDA MASH drug could have a huge impact on helping such patients. However, this remains to be seen upon the completion of a soon to be initiated phase 1 study for this program.

With positive results from the phase 3 China study of denifanstat for the treatment of patients with acne vulgaris, plus the ability to advance a MASH treatment combination study, I believe that investors could benefit from any potential gains made here.

data.title: I Prefer Bank of America Over Citigroup After Berkshire Hathaway Divestitures
data.description:
BAC and C Stock: Warren Buffett Kept Selling in Q1

I last covered Bank of America Corporation (NYSE:BAC) back in November 2024. That article was titled "Bank of America And JPMorgan Chase: Deregulation Tailwinds Ahead”. The article rated BAC as a buy based on the potential deregulation expected from the upcoming Trump administration at that time. As for Citigroup (NYSE:C) stock, my last coverage dates further back to about a year ago in June 2024. In an article titled “Citigroup Remains A Buy With The 2024 Stress Test Results”, I also rated the stock as a buy considering C’s strong results from the stressing test at that time.

Since then, much has changed both in terms of macroscopic parameters and also business operations for both banks. In the remainder of this article, I will reexamine both stocks, with a focus on the top 2 changes in my mind: the divestitures reported in Berkshire Hathaway’s recent 13F disclosures and also the worsening delinquency rates. In the end, this reexamination has led me to adjust my rating on C to hold while maintaining my buy rating on BAC.

Let me start with the Berkshire Hathaway’s 13F disclosures. As seen in the chart below, Warren Buffett continued to trim BRK’s BAC position. In Q1 of 2025, BRK reduced its BAC position from 680 million shares in the previous quarter to about 631 million. This is a reduction of almost 50 million shares in one quarter, or about 7.15% in percentages. Together with BAC, BRK has also reduced its exposure to other banking stocks. The 13F disclosure shows that BRK has completely exited its C position in the past quarter and also reduced its exposure to Capital One Financial (COF).

Next, I will explain why I see good reasons behind BRK’s above divestitures.

BAC and C Stock: Macroeconomic Risks

In a nutshell, my view is that the current macroeconomic landscape entails far greater uncertainties for the banking sector than at the time of my last writings. As an example, the next chart displays the percentage of US Auto Loans Delinquent by 90 or More Days (I:USALD90) in the past 10 years since 2016. As seen, the delinquency rate, currently at 4.99%, has been on a general upward trend in the past 1~2 years. The current level is among the peak levels in this period and far above the level of ~3.20% observed in 2016.

Other indicators are equally concerning. As a second example, the next chart illustrates the Delinquency Rate on Credit Card Loans for All Commercial Banks in the past decade. As seen, the reading has also marched upward substantially since my last writings. To wit, as of Q1 2025, the delinquency rate is 3.05%, also hovering around the peak levels in at least 10 years and is far above the pre-pandemic levels of ~2.5%.

Looking ahead, I see several factors that could further stretch consumer wallets and drive up delinquency rates. The ongoing tariff negotiations are unlikely to end up with tariff rates comparable to those before the “liberation day” in my view; thus I expect high overall costs and prices for consumer goods. A particular example is that President Trump just announced the doubling of tariff rates on imported steel and aluminum (see the Times news quoted below). I expect this to drive up the costs of both metals in the U.S. market. Both materials are essential for a wide range of consumer goods, with automobiles as a notable example sector.

Time (June 1, 2025) news: President Donald Trump announced on Friday that he plans to double the tariffs on steel and aluminum imports—increasing the charge from 25% to 50%. The steepened tariffs could potentially further escalate tensions between the U.S. and its top steel partners, which include Canada, Brazil, and Mexico.

BAC and C Stock: Valuation and Profitability

With the above macroscopic backdrop, it is a very sensible move in my mind for BRK to trim its exposure to banking stocks. Specific to BAC and C, BAC is the more expensively valued stock, but I think it is more expensive for good reasons. To wit, the chart below presents a valuation comparison between BAC and C stocks based on various P/E metrics. My overall assessment is the BAC is valuated at a premium of around 15% to 20% over C. For example, in terms of their FY1 P/E ratios, BAC’s ratio is 12.24, about 18% above C's ratio of 10.37. Looking further ahead to FY2 and FY2, BAC's P/E ratios show a similar premium as seen.

However, I believe the above valuation premium is well justified for several reasons. The top one on my list is BAC’s superior profitability. As an example, the chart below illustrates the Return on Equity (ROE) for both BAC and C stocks in the past 5 years from 2021 to the present. As seen, BAC has consistently maintained a higher ROE than C since 2021. Its average ROE in the past 5 years has been 9.52% compared to C's average of 7.10%. Currently, Bank of America's ROE hovers around 9.44%, while Citigroup's is 6.37% only.

Other Risks and Final Thoughts

Besides superior profitability metrics, BAC is also more conservatively leveraged in my model, a key factor to consider in times of uncertainty. As a reflection, the following chart illustrates the Assets To Shareholder Equity ratio on a quarterly basis in the past 5 years. As seen, over the period shown, Citigroup has generally maintained a higher ratio, with an average of 11.67, while Bank of America's average stands at 11.10. Currently, Citigroup has a ratio of 12.11, both noticeably above its own historical average and also above Bank of America’s reading of 11.33.

In terms of upside risks, C’s more leverage model could amplify its gains also in case the macroeconomy conditions improve. A potential scenario is for the central bank to gradually lower interest rates. This could help to decrease consumers' borrowing costs in tandem and reverse the increase in delinquency rates as aforementioned. The current market outlook seems to suggest this scenario as you can tell from the data provided by the CME Group FedWatch Tool. Currently, the most likely scenario the market expects is for the Federal Reserve to reduce rates to the 3.75% to 4.0% range by the end of 2025.

But let me close by pointing out two caveats. First, the market outlook for further rate cuts has become less optimistic with recent developments (such as inflation data, trade conflicts, and also the recent downgrade of the U.S. credit rating). To wit, even a month ago, the market’s most expected scenario pointed to deeper rate cuts (to 3.5%~3.75% range) as seen. Second, lower rates – even if materialized as the market expects – can only benefit some of BAC and C’s segments and may generate mixed results for them. Generally, lower rates tend to pressure the net interest income for their commercial banking operations.

All told, this follow-up analysis on BAC and C is primarily triggered by two catalysts since my last writings. The first one is the divestitures reported by BRK in Q1 2025 and the second one is the worsening delinquency rates on various consumer loans. With the ongoing geopolitical and macroeconomic risks, I see large odds for the delinquency rates to persist at elevated levels or even further worsen. Under these conditions, I see BAC as a better choice given its better ROE and lower leverage compared to C. I expect these factors to make BAC more resilient, no matter what comes next. These considerations have led me to downgrade my rating on C to hold while maintaining my buy rating on BAC.

data.title: Weak Jobs And Rising Inflation Fuel Stagflation Fears
data.description:
The ADP report and the ISM services reports were both shockingly weaker than expected. This came as a big surprise compared to what analysts had been expecting and will put some extra weight on the job report on Friday.

The ADP private payroll rose by only 37,000 in May, missing estimates of 110,000 and marking the weakest increase since March 2023. Meanwhile, the ISM services report came in at 49.9 versus forecasts for 52.0. Additionally, the new order index fell to 46.4 from 52.3 in April, while the price paid index rose to 68.7 from 65.1. The ISM services report notes that a 49.9 on the overall index corresponds to a 0.4% real GDP growth rate.

The data today was just not good, and again speaks of inflationary pressures, a weaker job market, and slower economic growth, which is a bit of a concern because it points to a stagflation-like environment developing in the economy. While it has not yet shown up in all the hard data, it seems to be a warning sign that higher inflation rates and slower job growth could be on the way.

Higher Inflation Rate

Historically, the services price paid index has tended to be a leading indicator of the Consumer Price Index (CPI). It would seem to suggest that the odds of inflation continuing to decline on a year-over-year basis have run their course and will likely begin to rise. While it is difficult to determine precisely what the inflation rate would look like, it would likely be closer to the upper end of the historical range for inflation, perhaps closer to 4% than the current 2.3%.

Current CPI swap prices suggest that the inflation rate is expected to rise back to 3.3%, and the recent prices paid data indicate that inflation appears to be well on its way to those higher inflation rates.

Weaker Job Growth

While the ADP report doesn't necessarily correlate with the non-farm payroll report due on Friday, it does suggest a slowdown in hiring in May. Whether that shows up in this month's non-farm payroll report is another question, just because the ADP data and the NFP can vary, sometimes significantly. As of now, Kalshi is pricing in 139,000 jobs to be created in May, which is down from around 150,000 at the end of May. Currently, analysts estimate that 130,000 were created in May.

Given this, it seems that more emphasis will be placed on this job report. While one month won't make a trend, it could cause some anxiety across the markets should it come in weaker than expected. The good news, of course, is that historically, at least on the first report, the Non-Farm payroll tends to beat the median estimates.

Certainly, a weaker-than-expected job report could result in interest rates across the Treasury curve falling, the dollar weakening, and, all things being equal, taking some froth off the recent rally in the stock market. But more importantly, it could reignite fears of slower growth, a weakening labor market, and higher inflation, which sounds a lot like stagflation, even if it isn't yet reflected in all the numbers.

data.title: Trump, Powell And Rates: Tariffs On, Tariffs Off
data.description:
By Kevin Flanagan & Samuel Rines

Obviously, headlines are swirling at a rather quick pace, so it is important to keep the lines of communication open. This blog is an updated installment to our series we have been publishing since the beginning of the year. Here are our latest thoughts following last week’s whirlwind news:

Trump

  • “Tariffs on, Tariffs off” and repeat. The past couple of weeks have seen a China “deal” coming out of meetings in Switzerland. And then it was EU tariffs being increased. Then it was EU tariffs off. Then it was China not playing by the rules. This is the tariff volatility to expect approaching the early July deadlines.
  • The One Big Beautiful Bill made its way through Congress, only to find significant opposition in the Senate. Something will get done over the summer, but the Senate is going to have their say in the meantime. The State and Local Tax deduction (SALT) is one of the more important consumer stimulus aspects of the bill, and the cap is currently set at $40,000. That is what to watch for whether or not the bill will be a tax cut for consumers into 2026.
  • Consumers have not pulled back—yet. While many of the surveys point to a deterioration in sentiment, the consumer has said one thing and done another. Earnings from companies ranging from Costco (COST) to Ulta (ULTA) have shown resilience, even strength. While not evenly spread across retailers, the impact of tariffs on U.S. consumer spending have not shown up in the results.
  • Looking forward, the volatility of tariffs and the uncertainty around the final language in the tax bill will dominate the headlines and markets. But these uncertainties are likely to be resolved in the coming months (the 90-day deadline for the tariff delay and the summer for the tax bill). Investors would be wise to look through the noise to the likely outcomes and prepare accordingly.

Powell

  • Fed Chair Powell met with President Trump in person for the first time since 2019. While reports indicate the President told Powell the Fed is making a mistake by not cutting rates, the Chair stuck with his messaging that future monetary policy decision-making will be strictly data-dependent.
  • Along those lines, recent Fed-speak has centered on the premise that the FOMC will not have enough information for a potential policy decision until September, at the earliest.
  • The June 18 Fed meeting will contain the Summary of Economic Projections, which includes the dot plot. It’s a close call whether the current median Fed Funds forecast remains at two rate cuts or is downshifted to perhaps only one.
  • The voting members will more than likely emphasize heightened uncertainty to the outlook with risks for higher unemployment and higher inflation both at play.
  • In our opinion, our baseline of one or two rate cuts for this year remains the reasonable case scenario.

Rates

  • After the initial selling, especially in longer-dated maturities, following Moody’s downgrade and the passage of the One Big Beautiful Bill, Treasury (UST) market trading has settled down a bit with 10 and 30-year yields now a few basis points lower than two weeks ago.
  • We feel concerns of Selling America are overblown, as last week’s slate of UST fixed and floating rate coupon auctions saw relatively solid domestic and foreign demand. Anxieties following the recent weak showing at the 20-year bond auction failed to take into consideration that this maturity has essentially been investors’ least favorite UST security since its inception.

Conclusion

Our advice remains: stay tuned and don’t get too caught up in the day-to-day headlines because this is going to be a very fluid situation, with lots of volatility.
